slots.static_text: sEMG studies were performed on the masseter, anterior temporalis, sternocleidomastoid, and trapezius muscles and confirmed:
Elevated muscular activity; with incoordination and aberrant function of the facial musculature was confirmed

The muscles of mastication were tested for their functional capacity measuring the maximum bite force and strength of these facial muscles. The measurements were objectively determined with the use of a gnathodynamometer. These measurements objectively determine the maximum bite force of the muscles of mastication used in chewing, comparing one side to the other. The muscles of mastication are innervated by the fifth cranial nerve, the Trigeminal Nerve. The Trigeminal Nerve has a motor function and a sensory function. The motor function of the Trigeminal Nerve controls the bite force of the muscles of mastication. The measurements obtained using a gnathodynamometer objectively demonstrate any unequal biting forces of the muscles bilaterally, and also correlate and confirm the applicant’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods by objectively quantifying the amount of the applicant’s bite force.

example_values: The objective measurements confirm Ms. Witt’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods because Ms. Witt could only apply the maximum bite force of 135 Newtons upon biting, when the normal amount of Newton forces to eat hard and chewy foods should exceed 400 Newtons. (400 Newtons = 90 Pounds Force)

The objective measurements confirm Mr. Lazo’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods because Mr. Lazo could only apply the maximum bite force of 118 Newtons upon biting, when the normal amount of Newton forces to eat hard and chewy foods should exceed 400 Newtons. (400 Newtons = 90 Pounds Force)

The objective measurements confirm Mr. Pina’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods because Mr. Pina could only apply the maximum bite force of 142 Newtons upon biting, when the normal amount of Newton forces to eat hard and chewy foods should exceed 400 Newtons. (400 Newtons = 90 Pounds Force)
"Gnathodynamometer--a reliable device for assessing jaw bite force."     Shushma, G., Kantly, R. Journal of Evolution of Medical and Dental Sciences, vol. 5, no. 102, 22 Dec. 2016, pp. 7488+

The objective measurements also confirm that Mr. Rivera has a weaker force of his bite on one side of his muscles of mastication as compared to the other side. The closing force of his right side measured at 4 Newtons, while closing force of the left side of his closing muscles of mastication was 3 Newtons.

The objective measurements also confirm that Mr. Pina has a weaker force of his bite on one side of his muscles of mastication as compared to the other side. The closing force of his right side measured at 142 Newtons, while closing force of the left side of his closing muscles of mastication was 140 Newtons.

slots.static_text: Section 4604.5(d) of the Labor Code states: “for all injuries not covered by the official utilization schedule adopted pursuant to Section 5307.27, authorized treatment shall be in accordance with other evidence-based medical treatment guidelines that are recognized generally by the national medical community and scientifically based.

The dental specialty of Orofacial Pain has been established by the American Dental Association, and its specialty association has published standard of care guidelines for the assessment, diagnosis, and management of Orofacial Pain in the following text:

Orofacial Pain Guidelines for Assessment, Diagnosis, and Management, Sixth Edition. Leeuw, Reny de, editor. | Klasser, Gary D., editor.  American Academy of Orofacial Pain.2018 Quintessence Publishing Co, Inc.
On page 170 it states:

“Conservative (ie. reversible), noninvasive treatment such as self-management instructions, behavioral modification, physical therapy, medications, and orthopedic appliances are endorsed for the initial care of nearly all TMDs.”
Greene CS. Managing the care of patients with temporomandibular disorders: A new guideline for care. J Am Dent Assoc 2010; 141:1086–1088
On page 181 of the Sixth Edition of the Orofacial Pain Guidelines for Assessment, Diagnosis. and Management published by the American Academy of Orofacial Pain, it states:

“Orthopedic appliances, including interocclusal splints, orthotics, orthoses, bite guards, bite planes, night guards, or bruxism appliances, are routinely used in the treatment of TMDs. Based on current theory, removable acrylic resin appliances that cover the teeth have traditionally been used to alter occlusal relationships and to redistribute occlusal forces, to prevent wear and mobility of the teeth, to reduce bruxism and parafunction, to treat masticatory muscle pain and dysfunction, to treat painful TMJs, and to alter structural relationships in the TMJ.”

slots.static_text: Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device for Daytime Use

The primary purpose of the Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device is to relieve facial muscle, ligament, and nerve impingement. This appliance serves to maintain the stomatognathic musculature at their proper resting length, from origin to insertion, thus decreasing pain and improving function. The appliance also decreases the inter-capsular joint pressure placed upon the TMJ from clenching and bracing of the facial musculature. The appliance also protects wearing down and fracture of the teeth from the extreme pressures placed on the teeth from bruxism. This appliance is fabricated and customized in our in-house dental laboratory with a multiple-day process inclusive of in-person adjustments as necessary.

"Traditionally, dentists treat bruxism with an oral appliance, generically called a night guard appliance. However, it has been documented that when a regular bruxism night guard appliance is given to a patient to wear during sleep, this appliance can exacerbate nighttime obstructions of the airway. Therefore, the standard of care is that a patient cannot use a daytime bruxism oral appliance while sleeping, day or night. Rather, patients must use an oral sleep appliance such as a mandibular advancement or tongue-retaining appliance during sleep. The oral sleep appliance serves to not only protect from the ill effects of bruxism, but also to bring the mandible and tongue forward, thereby, promoting opening of the airway."  Journal of the California Dental Association, August 2016. Volume 44. No. 8 page 510
